text: Leniency
Concession
Leverage
Waive
Suspended
Custodial
Stipulation
Downgrade
Forgo
Counterpart
Cooperate
Lesser charge
My client would rather go to trial than accept those terms.
We would prefer probation to any custodial sentence.
We will plead guilty, provided the charge is downgraded.
We will not settle unless the sentence is suspended.
Counsel, here is our offer: a guilty plea to the full charge, four years in custody. Take it, or we go to trial.
Your evidence is thin, and we both know it. My client would rather go to trial than accept a custodial sentence on that count. That is my leverage.
Then make me a concession. What is your client willing to give?
He will plead to the lesser charge and make full restitution, provided you downgrade the principal count. We would prefer probation to any time in custody.
Probation is a stretch. I could offer a suspended sentence, but only if he agrees to cooperate and name his counterpart.
We will accept a suspended sentence as long as he keeps his right to appeal. He will not waive that, even if it costs us the deal.
Then we have a deal: lesser charge, suspended sentence, full restitution as a stipulation. I will forgo the second count.

A plea bargain is where most criminal cases are really settled, and as a defender you must master the table, not only the courtroom. The prosecution will open with a hard offer; your job is to test it, not to take it. First, weigh the strength of the case against you: if the evidence is thin, you would rather go to trial than concede too much. Second, protect your client: never accept a deal unless it serves them better than the risk of a verdict. Third, trade, do not surrender. You might say you would accept the lesser charge provided the sentence is suspended, or that your client would prefer probation to any time in custody. Hold one concession back as leverage. And remember: as long as the offer is on the table, you keep talking; the moment it is withdrawn, you are back before the bench. A good defender knows when to bargain and when to walk away.

You are listening to the legal affairs review. A long-running fraud case ended this week not with a verdict but with a negotiated plea. Sources say the defence had signalled it would rather face trial than accept the original terms, which carried a custodial sentence. After several rounds of talks, the prosecution agreed to reduce the principal charge, provided the accused entered a guilty plea on a lesser count and made full restitution to the victims. The defence, for its part, had pressed for a suspended sentence and would not settle unless probation replaced any time in custody. The final deal, approved by the court, spared the accused prison as long as the agreed conditions were met. Both sides described the outcome as a workable compromise rather than a defeat.
